data.title: From Lloyds to Polymarket: Prediction Markets Are Reshaping the Insurance Industry
data.description:
Author:Vinko EditorSleepy.txt

In 2023, a letter was sent to the mailboxes of 100,000 households in Florida, USA.

The letter came from Farmers Insurance, a century-old insurance company. Its content was brief and brutal: 100,000 insurance policies, from homes to cars, were voided immediately.

A written promise, made in black and white, became worthless overnight. Angry policyholders flooded social media, questioning the company they had trusted for decades. But all they received was a cold announcement: "We must more effectively control our risk exposure." In California, the situation is even worse. Insurance giants like State Farm and Allstate have stopped accepting any new home insurance applications, and over 2.8 million existing policies have been refused renewal. An unprecedented "insurance retreat" is unfolding in the United States. The insurance industry, once a social stabilizer and a safety net for everyone, has itself fallen into turmoil. Why? Let's look at the following data. Hurricane Helene's damage to North Carolina may exceed $53 billion; Hurricane Milton, according to Goldman Sachs, could result in insurance losses exceeding $25 billion; and for the Los Angeles fire, AccuWeather estimates total economic losses at $250 billion to $275 billion, while CoreLogic estimates insurance payouts at between $35 billion and $45 billion. Insurance companies are finding themselves facing the limits of their payout capacity. So, who can replace the traditional insurance industry? The story begins in London more than three hundred years ago. In 1688, on the banks of the Thames, in a café called Lloyd's, sailors, merchants, and ship owners were all under the same shadow. Merchant ships laden with cargo were sailing from London to distant America or Asia. A safe return meant enormous wealth; but if they encountered storms, pirates, or ran aground, they would lose everything. Risk, like a persistent dark cloud, hung over the hearts of every seafarer. The café owner, Edward Lloyd, was a shrewd businessman. He realized that these captains and cargo owners needed more than just coffee; they needed a place to share the risks. So, he began to encourage a kind of "betting game." A captain wrote down information about his ship and cargo on a piece of paper and posted it on the wall of a café. Anyone willing to take a portion of the risk could sign their name on the paper and indicate the amount they were willing to insure. If the ship returned safely, they would share a portion of the captain's payment (i.e., the premium); if the ship sank, they would compensate the captain for his losses proportionally. If the ship returned, everyone was happy; if the ship sank, the loss was shared. This was the prototype of modern insurance. It didn't have complex actuarial models, only simple business wisdom—distributing one person's enormous risk among a group of people to share. In 1774, 79 insurers united to form Lloyd's of London, moving from a coffee shop into the Royal Exchange. A trillion-dollar modern financial industry was born. For over three centuries, the essence of the insurance industry has remained unchanged: it is a business of managing risk. Through actuarial calculations, the probability of various risks occurring is determined, risks are priced, and then sold to those seeking protection. But today, this ancient business model faces unprecedented challenges. When the frequency and intensity of hurricanes, floods, and wildfires far exceed the predictions of historical data and actuarial models, insurance companies find that their existing methods are no longer sufficient to measure the world's growing uncertainty. They have only two options: either drastically increase premiums, or, as we've seen in Florida and California—retreat. A More Elegant Solution: Risk Hedging When the insurance industry is caught in a dilemma of "inaccurate calculations, inability to pay out, and reluctance to insure," we might as well step outside the insurance framework and look for answers in another ancient industry: finance. In 1983, McDonald's planned to launch a revolutionary product: Chicken McNuggets. But a problem arose for management: chicken prices fluctuated too much. If menu prices were locked in, the company would face huge losses if chicken prices skyrocketed. The tricky part was that there was no chicken futures market at the time to hedge against the risk. Ray Dalio, then a commodities trader, offered a brilliant solution. He told McDonald's chicken suppliers, "The cost of a chicken is just the chick, corn, and soybean meal, right? The price of chicks is relatively stable; what really fluctuates are the prices of corn and soybean meal. You can buy corn and soybean meal futures contracts on the futures market to lock in production costs, thus providing McDonald's with chicken at a fixed price." This seemingly normal "synthetic futures" idea was revolutionary at the time. It not only helped McDonald's successfully launch Chicken McNuggets but also laid the groundwork for Ray Dalio's later founding of Bridgewater Associates, the world's largest hedge fund. Another classic example comes from Southwest Airlines. In 1993, then-CFO Gary Kelly began establishing a fuel hedging strategy for the company. From 1998 to 2008, this strategy saved Southwest Airlines approximately $3.5 billion in fuel costs, equivalent to 83% of the company's profits during the same period. During the 2008 financial crisis, when oil prices soared to $130 per barrel, Southwest Airlines purchased 70% of its fuel through futures contracts at a locked-in price of $51 per barrel. This made it the only major US airline at the time to maintain its "free baggage policy." Whether it's McDonald's chicken or Southwest Airlines' fuel, both reveal the same simple business wisdom: transforming future uncertainty into present certainty through financial markets. This is hedging. It shares the same goal as insurance, but the underlying logic is completely different. Insurance is the transfer of risk. You transfer risk (such as a car accident or illness) to an insurance company and pay premiums for it; hedging is the offsetting of risk. You have a position in the spot market (for example, needing to buy fuel), and you establish an opposite position in the futures market (for example, buying fuel futures). When the spot price rises, the profit in the futures can offset the loss in the spot market. Insurance is a relatively closed system, dominated by insurance companies and actuaries; hedging, on the other hand, is an open system where prices are jointly set by market participants. So, since hedging is so elegant and efficient, why can't we use it to solve the current predicament of the insurance industry? Why can't a resident of Florida hedge the risk of a hurricane landfall like Southwest Airlines? The answer is simple: because there isn't such a market. Until a young man who started a business in the bathroom brought it to us. From "Risk Transfer" to "Risk Trading" 22-year-old Shayne Coplan founded Polymarket in his bathroom. This blockchain-based prediction market gained fame in 2024 due to the US presidential election, with annual trading volume exceeding $9 billion.

Besides the political betting, Polymarket has some very interesting markets. For example, will Houston's high temperature exceed 105 degrees Fahrenheit in August? Will California's nitrogen dioxide concentration be above average this week?

An anonymous trader named Neobrother has made over $20,000 by trading these weather contracts on Polymarket. He and his followers are known as "weather hunters."

While insurance companies fled Florida because they couldn't predict the weather, a group of mysterious players were enthusiastically trading temperature differences of 0.1 degrees Celsius. Prediction markets, in essence, are platforms where "everything can be futures-ized." They expand the function of traditional futures markets from standardized commodities (oil, corn, foreign exchange) to any publicly verifiable and objectively verifiable event. This provides a completely new approach to solving the insurance industry's dilemma. First, it replaces the arrogance of experts with the wisdom of the collective. Traditional insurance pricing relies on insurance companies' actuarial models. However, as the world becomes increasingly unpredictable, models based on historical data fail. Predicting market prices, on the other hand, is determined by the "votes" of thousands of participants with their real money. It reflects the sum of information the market has about the probability of an event occurring. The price fluctuation of a contract regarding whether a hurricane will make landfall in Florida in May is itself the most sensitive and real-time measure of risk. Secondly, it replaces the helplessness of bearing losses with the freedom of trading. If a Florida resident is worried about their home being destroyed by a hurricane, they no longer only have the option of "buying insurance." They can look into the market and buy a contract predicting a hurricane's arrival. If the hurricane does hit, the profit from the contract can be used to cover the cost of the damaged home. This is essentially a personalized form of risk hedging. More importantly, they can sell the contract at any time to lock in profits or cut losses. Risk is no longer a heavy burden that needs to be packaged and transferred all at once, but rather an asset that can be segmented, traded, and bought and sold at any time. They have transformed from a risk-taker into a risk trader. This is not just a technological improvement, but a renewal of mindset. It liberates the pricing power of risk from a few elite institutions and returns it to everyone. The End of Insurance, or a New Beginning? Will Prediction Markets, this "Universal Risk Trading Platform," Replace Insurance? On the one hand, prediction markets are eroding the foundation of the traditional insurance industry in a fundamental way. The core of the traditional insurance industry is information asymmetry. Insurance companies possess actuaries and massive data models; they need to understand risk better than you to price it. But when the pricing power of risk is replaced by an open, transparent market driven by collective wisdom or even insider information, the information advantage of insurance companies vanishes. Residents of Florida no longer need to blindly trust insurance company quotes; a glance at hurricane contract prices on Polymarket reveals the market's true assessment of risk. More importantly, traditional insurance is a "heavy model"—sales, underwriting, loss assessment, claims processing…every step is fraught with labor costs and friction; while prediction markets are an extremely "light model," focusing solely on transactions and settlements, with virtually no intermediate steps. However, on the other hand, we see that prediction markets are not a panacea; they cannot completely replace insurance. They can only hedge against objective risks that can be clearly defined and publicly verified (such as weather or election results). However, it falls short when it comes to more complex and subjective risks (such as accidents caused by driving behavior or personal health conditions). You can't open a contract on Polymarket and have the whole world predict "whether you'll have a car accident next year." Personalized risk assessment and management remain the core strength of the traditional insurance industry. The future landscape may not be a war of annihilation where "who replaces whom," but rather a new and sophisticated competitive and cooperative relationship. Prediction markets will become the infrastructure for risk pricing. Like Bloomberg Terminal and Reuters today, they provide the most basic data anchors for the financial world. Insurance companies may also become deep participants in prediction markets, using market prices to calibrate their models or hedge against catastrophic risks they cannot handle. Insurance companies will return to the essence of service. When pricing advantages disappear, insurance companies must rethink their value. Their core competitiveness will no longer be information asymmetry, but rather a focus on risk areas requiring deep involvement, personalized management, and long-term services, such as health management, retirement planning, and wealth transfer. The behemoths of the old world are learning the dance of the new world. And the explorers of the new world need to find routes back to the old world. Epilogue: Over three hundred years ago, in a London coffeehouse, a group of merchants, with the most primitive wisdom, invented the mechanism of risk-sharing. Over three hundred years later, in the digital world, players are reshaping how we deal with risk. History always seems to cycle unexpectedly. From forced trust to free trading, this may be another exciting moment in financial history. Each of us will evolve from a passive risk recipient to an active risk manager. And this is not just about insurance, but about how each of us can better survive in this uncertain world.

data.title: Why did the US Supreme Court target Trump?
data.description:
Author: Zhibenshe

Just now, the U.S. Supreme Court ruled 6-3 to reject Trump's global tariff policy.
This is the biggest policy setback Trump has faced since taking office.
Trump responded immediately, signing an order imposing a 10% tariff on imports from all countries for 150 days.
This ruling restricts Trump's tariff weapon, leaving him with only four tariff cards: the familiar Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 and Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974; and the unused Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974 and Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930.
What were the Supreme Court's reasons for this ruling?

First, IEEPA grants the president the power to "regulate imports," including "investigating, freezing during investigations, monitoring, directing and enforcing, repealing, declaring invalid, preventing or prohibiting imports or exports," but without any mention of tariffs or duties. In other words, Trump can prohibit imports based on IEEPA, but cannot impose tariffs. Unfortunately, when cases reach the Supreme Court, it's all about wording; without legal authorization, nothing can be done. Second, IEEPA is activated under national emergency conditions, and currently the United States is not at war with any country in the world. The US government must acknowledge that the president is acting in peacetime. The Supreme Court does not inherently possess the power to impose tariffs. However, a careful reading of the full ruling reveals a deeper reason: to limit President Trump's power. Trump's actions since taking office have caused concern in the United States and the international community. The Democratic Party, Congress, and other governments all hope for a force to restrain Trump. The U.S. Constitution establishes a political system of separation of powers, and the Supreme Court justices' duty is to uphold the Constitution. A ruling based on the Constitution is the best way to limit Trump's power. The justices cited Article I, Section 8, Paragraph 1 of the Constitution, which states: "Congress shall have power to determine and to impose taxes, duties, import duties, and excise duties." Alexander Hamilton explained that the power to tax was "the most important of all the powers to be granted to the Union." The framers of the Constitution recognized the unique importance of the power to tax, therefore they stipulated that "only" Congress had the "authority to take from the people's property," that is, "no taxation without representation" (Federalist Paper No. 48, p. 310, J. Madison). Article I, Section 7, Paragraph 1 of the U.S. Constitution ensured that only the House of Representatives could "propose supplies necessary to support the government, thereby reducing all the excessive privileges of the other branches." (The Declaration of Independence, 19th...) The article requires that "all bills imposing taxes should be introduced by the House of Representatives." The justices held that the "broadness of authority" currently claimed by the president "strongly demonstrates" that these tariffs exceed the president's legitimate powers. This ruling shows that this president is not an emperor, and Trump cannot escape the constraints of the American system. This has also led many to believe again that the American system is effective. Finally, I raise a question: What are the problems with the American system? This is the question that anyone concerned about or worried about the progress of human civilization truly needs to consider.

data.title: Will agents "transactions be conducted using cryptocurrency"?
data.description:
Foreword

For some time now, I've been intensively integrating AI into the development processes of multiple real-world projects: from prototype design, code generation, and task breakdown to automated debugging and documentation generation, more and more work is being organized and executed in the form of agents.

In this state of "working alongside AI," a very intuitive feeling is that agents are no longer just tools, but are gradually becoming "production units" that can collaborate, divide tasks, and even call upon each other's capabilities. As my understanding deepens, I've begun to frequently see another type of discussion gaining momentum:

How will settlements be made between agents in the future? Will it naturally lead to blockchain or cryptocurrency?

These discussions sound logical, yet something subtly feels amiss. Therefore, this article is more like a thought experiment: attempting to temporarily set aside the existing financial framework and rethink, starting from the computation and system structure itself—if agents truly become productivity nodes, what exactly are they "exchanging"? In the past year or two, there has been increasing discussion about AI Agents, and a very common assertion is that Agents will become independent economic entities in the future, and transactions between them will be completed using blockchain and cryptocurrencies. This statement sounds natural—since it's "economic activity between machines," using "decentralized currency" for settlement seems logical. But if we break the problem down to the technological level, we'll find something counterintuitive: what agents really need might not be a "payment system" at all. Instead, it's a completely new infrastructure—a protocol layer used to settle "intelligent labor" itself. I. We are mistakenly comparing Agents to "people," but they are more like "processes." Most of today's imaginations about the Agent economy draw parallels to human society: Human-to-human transactions → Agent-to-Agent transactions; Humans use currency for settlement → Agents also use currency for settlement; Humans need banks → Agents need on-chain wallets. The problem is that this analogy fails at the most crucial point. Human transactions, in essence, are about solving one thing: how to complete the exchange of value when the future fulfillment of the contract is uncertain. Therefore, we need: Accounts, Balances, Settlement, Credit, Accounting System. However, the collaboration between Agents follows a completely different logic. The agent doesn't "promise future delivery"; it only does one thing: perform a calculation and then immediately provide the result. What is this more like? It's not like a contract between companies, but more like two processes in an operating system calling each other. II. Agents exchange "completed work," not "assets." Imagine a future collaborative scenario: A decision agent needs to complete a complex prediction within 10ms, so it sends a request to the network: Who can complete this inference within the time limit? What is the required accuracy?

  • What is the maximum allowed consumption of computing resources?

Multiple specialized agents quote, execute, and return results.

Multiple specialized agents quote, execute, and return results.

The key point is here: The core of this "transaction" isn't money, but rather—whether this calculation was actually executed, whether the result is verifiable, and whether the resources consumed are measurable. In other words, what agents care about is: Instead of: State Consensus, this is something outside the types of problems that blockchain excels at solving. III. Why do many of the advantages of blockchain become burdens in the agent world? The design goal of blockchain is to establish trust for "asset transfers between strangers". However, the characteristic of Agent collaboration is: If every model call, feature calculation, and intermediate inference result is written onto the blockchain, it's equivalent to: Recording every CPU register operation in a bank statement. The system will crash directly due to throughput issues. Fourth, what's more likely to emerge in the future is not Machine Payment, but Work Settlement. What agents truly need is a new mechanism: proving that you have completed the work, which is itself equivalent to settlement. This can be understood as a kind of: a "semantic upgrade" of Proof-of-Work. However, the "Work" here is not mining, but rather: a single inference; an optimization solution; and a data transformation. leaf="">A controlled use of computing power

When execution can be verified:

The payment behavior is "compressed" into the execution itself and no longer needs to exist independently.

This is not: Doing → Paying. Rather: Proof of doing = Payment completed. Today's market is: First comes the goods, then the price is discovered. The Agent world is more like: First comes the task, supply is generated in real time, and price is formed instantly. There is no long-term order book, no inventory, and every demand generates a "micro-market" that exists for only tens of milliseconds. It's closer to: Cloud Computing Scheduling Kubernetes Resource Allocation CDN Real-Time Bidding Rather than a Stock Exchange. Sixth, this system actually reduces computing power waste, not increases it. Many people worry that the Agent economy will devour more computing power, but the opposite is true. The biggest waste in AI today comes from: Repeated inference; Unreusable intermediate results; Over-reliance on large models; Unshareable computational results. If the execution results can be verified, measured, and reused, then: Already calculated... Embedding doesn't need to be recalculated. The newly completed feature space can be directly "purchased and used." Accuracy/latency/cost become real-time optimization problems for the first time. Intelligence transforms from a disposable consumable into a schedulable resource. VII. Blockchain may exist, but only at the "boundary layer of the human world." This doesn't mean blockchain has no place. It is more likely to exist in the following locations: Long-term clearing between different organizations; Data license registration; SLA guarantee; Cross-jurisdictional reconciliation. In other words: Human Economy ↔ Agent Economy The bridging layer, rather than the real-time execution layer of Agent-to-Agent.

VIII. The truly missing infrastructure has not yet been invented

For the Agent economy to succeed, the key is not in tokens or wallets, but in three capabilities that are still immature today:

1. Measurable computation standards (Meterable Intelligence)

2. Verifiable execution results (Verifiable Execution)

3. Ultra-low latency capability discovery and matching protocols

data.title: Prediction Markets: A Casino for Everything?
data.description:
Foreword

Looking ahead to February 2026, if we were to assess which areas of the Web3 industry have already entered the mainstream, stablecoins and prediction markets would likely be the only two answers.

Stablecoins, needless to say, have become a key focus for governments, central banks, top financial institutions, and Fintech companies worldwide. Major events in 2025 include Circle's IPO, Stripe's acquisition of the stablecoin platform Bridge for approximately $1.1 billion, Trump's signing of the GENIUS Act, Visa, Mastercard, Citibank, and Coinbase's bidding war for stablecoin technology company BVNK, and Hong Kong's push for stablecoin regulations. The total transaction volume of stablecoins is expected to exceed $40 trillion in 2025, with a total market capitalization exceeding $300 billion. It can be said that stablecoins are becoming a conduit and pillar of the new financial system.

Are prediction markets casinos?

Both Polymarket and Kalshi claim that "event contracts" are compliant financial derivatives and are seeking full regulatory approval (such as from the CFTC).

Kalshi only serves US users, while Polymarket, although a global marketplace, currently restricts access to 33 countries, according to Polymarket's geo-blocking FAQ. Most jurisdictions restricting Polymarket, such as the EU, France, Poland, Singapore, and Portugal, classify it as a form of gambling, requiring specific licenses, which are often incompatible with Polymarket's decentralized, natively encrypted business model. Even in some US states, such as New York, there are proposed bans on prediction markets to avoid competition with state-owned exclusive betting licensees. According to the "Prediction Markets Report" issued by Keyrock in conjunction with platforms such as Dune, specifically regarding prediction market betting events, in 2025, Kalshi was primarily focused on sports, with sports accounting for approximately 85% of its trading volume. In contrast, Polymarket exhibits a more diversified mix—sports (39%), politics (34%), and cryptocurrency (18%), but sports still dominate. This highly overlaps with the sports betting industry. Analyzing users further, while Polymarket's users are predominantly crypto enthusiasts, a recent study, "Gambling on Crypto Tokens?", provides strong evidence of a link between crypto assets and gambling behavior. Researchers used Google Trends to proxy retail investor attention, revealing several significant patterns: Regions with high per capita lottery sales (DMA) in the US showed significantly higher Google search activity related to ICOs, indicating a high degree of overlap in behavior between gamblers and crypto retail investors; in regions with high per capita lottery sales, wallet activity surged rapidly after an ICO or NFT launch; and when gambling became legal in a state, crypto-related attention significantly decreased in high-lottery-sales regions. This study also indirectly suggests that users on Polymarket currently have a stronger tendency towards gambling. Meme coin players on the Solana chain (P-Jiang) are generally considered to have a stronger gambling tendency. In 2025, nearly 99% of Meme coins issued on the Pump.fun platform had gone to zero, and Solana's transaction fee revenue also dropped by more than 90%. The decline of Meme coins coincides with the rise of prediction markets. Applying this to our research, we can reasonably suspect that former P-Jiang players are becoming new users of Polymarket. Moreover, compared to speculating on Meme coins, prediction markets are more transparent and fair in their mechanisms. When P-Jiang buys event contracts, the final win or loss depends on the objective outcome of the event, without the risk of project-driven manipulation. In conclusion, although there is controversy regarding whether prediction markets are casinos in terms of regulation, due to the high degree of overlap between prediction content and the sports betting industry, and the fact that users are more inclined to gamble, the author believes that prediction markets at the current juncture can be seen as a form of "duck typing" for casinos (betting companies). "If it looks like a duck and quacks like a duck, then it must be a duck." In fact, prediction markets have advantages over traditional casinos: First, events in prediction markets are tradable during the event (early payouts/profit-taking); second, in casinos, the bookmaker unilaterally sets the odds and is the only counterparty, while in prediction markets, the counterparties are other users, so the platform doesn't bear the risk of gambling against them; finally, under the mechanism of prediction markets, even very niche long-tail bets can be successfully launched, such as "betting on whether it will rain in Beijing tomorrow." Regarding the last point, the potential of prediction markets clearly extends beyond the scope of "gambling." Prediction markets can create markets for hedging financial and macroeconomic events, such as allowing small and medium-sized business owners (like farmers and importers/exporters) to hedge against "supply chain risks" (such as whether a port will close) or "policy risks" (such as tariff changes). The Problem with Prediction Markets – Market Making Difficulty As an exchange, to ensure smooth trading, the exchange must guarantee the availability of contracts at all times. This requires a stable source of liquidity – market makers. Market makers profit from the bid-ask spread, that is, buying low and selling high. In the conventional cryptocurrency trading market, if the timeframe is extended, mainstream assets will still exhibit a certain degree of fluctuation, and the upward and downward trends often rotate in cycles. Therefore, market makers can continuously make money in the cryptocurrency trading market. The trading instruments in prediction markets are essentially event contracts. Each contract has a specific settlement time, and the formula "Yes + No = 1" means that only one contract will ultimately have a value of 1 USDC, while the others will become zero. This means that bets in prediction markets will eventually end rapidly in an extreme one-sided market trend from a certain point in time, leaving market makers with very little time to react. To consistently make money, market makers need to capture profit opportunities as much as possible and avoid inventory risk when one-sided market trends occur. Market making is far more difficult than in the conventional cryptocurrency trading market. The above discussion about the difficulty of market making only applies to events with huge trading volumes (US elections, high-profile sporting events). Niche long-tail trading has almost no activity and exhibits extremely wide bid-ask spreads, making it almost impossible for market makers to profit in such situations. Furthermore, some events involve numerous insiders with close ties to or who are themselves sources of information. Market makers are naturally at an informational disadvantage against these players, and the liquidity they provide becomes a channel for these insiders to cash out, inevitably leading to losses for market makers. The difficulty of market making means limitations in liquidity creation, which directly impacts the user's trading experience. To address this issue, Polymarket and Kalshi, in addition to returning a portion or even all of their commissions to market makers, have also invested heavily in liquidity subsidies to attract more market makers. Polymarket has invested approximately $10 million in liquidity subsidies, and Kalshi has invested at least $9 million. In fact, market-making subsidies have become a solid competitive advantage for leading platforms like Polymarket and Kalshi, which is why other prediction markets have struggled to succeed. Without market-making subsidies, market-making can only be sustained through transaction fee revenue, requiring increased trading volume and fee income, which the current size of prediction markets is far from sufficient. In conclusion, although many proponents define prediction markets as decentralized information aggregation and pricing systems, or "truth machines," current prediction markets are clearly overly focused on sports betting and short-term cryptocurrency price manipulation—a "dopamine casino"—lacking long-term social information value. The author is not against prediction markets; on the contrary, the author is a Polymarket user. The growth of prediction markets essentially stems from people's gambling demand on "events"—whether political events, sporting matches, or reality itself. This demand is real and enduring, and we don't need to cloak it in any way, much less label it as a "truth machine." In fact, many financial activities in human society originate from gambling, only with clearer regulations, more explicit ethics, and more rational designs. Prediction markets have the potential to become a more widespread financial tool; institutions use them to hedge against uncertainty, retail investors use them to reap dopamine, insider traders use them to sell the truth, and AI uses them for quantitative trading. Welcome to prediction markets!

data.title: How AI can improve cryptography research output
data.description:
Author: Stacy Muur Translator: Shan Ouba, Jinse Finance

Crypto research only becomes chaotic when you don't set a lifecycle for it. Once I established this process, the noise decreased. Not to zero, but enough to allow for clear thinking. This is a framework I use repeatedly.

Discovery

I always start by identifying the question I truly want to answer. This sounds obvious, but it's a step most people rush through. I define the assets, protocols, narratives, or events I'm researching and write down the hypotheses I want to test. Not conclusions, but hypotheses.

Sometimes inspiration comes from my own curiosity. Other times, I look at projects that top VCs are investing in or experience a new protocol firsthand. Either way, this step sets boundaries for me.

Without it, I'd end up just aimlessly browsing through dashboards and posts, seemingly efficient but ultimately fruitless. Data Aggregation Once I know what I'm looking for, I gather raw signals from all the important channels. On-chain data, such as transactions, TVL, and whale wallet flows. Market data, such as price, volume, and derivatives positions. Social data from Twitter, Discord, and Reddit. And then there are the slower-paced sources: white papers, documents, and long-form research. In practice, this means using tools like Treeofalpha or WatcherGuru for news and CoinGecko/CoinMarketCap for historical price data. I don't spend too much time scrolling through crypto Twitter because if information has already circulated on Twitter, it's likely already diluted. The data is messy, but it's precisely in this mess that early signals often emerge.

Verification and Standardization

In this step, I conduct due diligence, cleanse the collected information, resolve inconsistencies, and align all content by time. I verify the data sources: comparing blockchain explorer data with paid APIs, and official dashboards with third-party aggregations.

Then I standardize the data: unifying units, time periods, and assumptions. If you skip this step, any patterns you find later may be illusions.

Pattern Recognition

Only after the data is cleaned will I start looking for patterns. Anomalies in on-chain behavior, sudden spikes in TVL, previously non-existent correlations, and sentiment shifts preceding price movements.

Tools are very helpful here. DefiLlama is excellent for discovering outliers at the ecosystem level.

Tools are very helpful here. DefiLlama is excellent for discovering outliers at the ecosystem level.

When I need a more specialized or protocol-specific view, I use Dune, Artemis, Dexu, and Token Terminal. The goal isn't to confirm biases, but to notice unusual aspects compared to the baseline. Narrative and Sentiment Mapping This is the step in translating numbers into meaning. If TVL rises, I ask why: new incentives? A points program? A genuine product breakthrough? Then I observe whether the market is genuinely discussing it, or if the data is subtly shifting. I recognize that prices and fundamentals don't move in isolation. They fluctuate along with narratives. Unraveling these narratives and determining whether they are reinforcing or fading is the bridge connecting analysis to reality. Integration and Reporting Finally, I bring everything together. I will write a concise core summary, highlighting key data, adding charts where necessary, and listing risks and opportunities. No fluff. The best report I've ever written is short enough to read quickly, yet insightful enough that every claim is traceable to evidence. This balance is difficult to strike. But once achieved, research is no longer overwhelming, but rather empowers decision-making. And this gives you a greater advantage than any single tool. Where AI Creates Real, Quantifiable Value I've tested enough AI tools to be inherently skeptical. But when AI truly works, the reasons are very specific. AI creates real, quantifiable value for crypto research and the wider ecosystem primarily in several key areas: addressing data scarcity, enhancing on-chain analytics, and automating complex processes through AI agents. These features also include: Data Aggregation and Standardization This is where AI truly shines. Modern large-model-driven systems can ingest data across public chains, browsers, APIs, news, encrypted Twitter, GitHub, and documents in a single workflow. What used to take days can now be done in minutes. More importantly, they automatically handle data standardization and time alignment. In my experience, this reduces data preparation by over 80%. And it provides something rare in the crypto space: a relatively consistent "fact baseline" view of what's happening right now. On-Chain Behavioral Pattern Recognition AI is particularly efficient at scaling. Wallet clustering, distinguishing between whales and retail investors, detecting abnormal liquidity changes or token release patterns, and mapping token transfers to reveal hidden connections between ecosystems—these are tasks that humans cannot efficiently repeat. When combined with compliance-grade tools, its capabilities become even more powerful. Not magical, but highly efficient. Natural Language Processing for Narrative and Sentiment Analysis This is the part that surprised me the most. Natural Language Processing models fine-tuned for the crypto space truly understand the language of the crypto industry. They can recognize sarcasm, track changes in governance sentiment, and capture narrative shifts before prices react, not afterward. The best systems ingest crypto tweets, Discord, forums, GitHub topics, blogs, and even podcasts. Some information finance platforms go a step further, weighting sentiment based on KOLs' credibility and historical accuracy. This isn't hype; it's signal filtering. Writing and Research Integration Even with perfect analysis, writing is still a task. Executive summaries, risk tables, comparison frameworks. These can take hours. AI won't replace judgment, but it excels at drafting and structuring. It gives me a clean foundation to refine, not a blank page to contend with. The real benefit isn't just speed, but consistency. Every report starts from the same analytical framework. AI Tool Ecosystem Overview I view the AI ​​tool ecosystem as a series of clearly defined layers, directly corresponding to my research methods. Each tool is adapted to a specific stage of my workflow, from raw data aggregation to integration and final research output.

1. AI-Driven Data Aggregation and Standardization

I use this layer to reduce setup costs and avoid wasting time cleaning data before the actual work begins.

  • Multi-chain data acquisition: Tools like SurfAI, Minara, and ChainGPT help me acquire data across multiple public chains for surface scanning and more in-depth protocol research.

    • Multi-chain data acquisition: Tools like SurfAI, Minara, and ChainGPT help me acquire data across multiple public chains for surface scanning and more in-depth protocol research.

      • Lower setup costs: These tools aggregate inputs for workflows such as wallet tracking, narrative monitoring, and cross-chain analytics, eliminating the need to switch between multiple dashboards. 2. On-chain pattern recognition and anomaly analysis: This is the step that transforms raw data into signals. Wallet clustering: Nansen and Arkham help me track smart money and associate addresses with known entities. Liquidity and release analysis: DefiLlama's LlamaAI allows me to query protocol-level metrics without manually switching between multiple dashboards. Cross-Ecosystem Fund Flow Mapping: LorisTools is my go-to tool for quickly viewing funding rates and capital flows across exchanges. 3. Natural Language Processing for Narrative, Sentiment, and Information Finance: I use NLP tools to understand how narratives change before prices fully react. Sentiment Influenced by KOL Weighting: KaitoAI helps me track narrative shifts, sentiment trends, and KOL activity in a structured way. Narrative Speed ​​vs. Price Movement Comparison: Grok is ideal for scanning crypto Twitter to discover emerging narratives early. 4. Writing, Integrating, and Research Output This is the final compression layer, transforming ideas into publishable research. Writing and Integrating: I rely on classic large models like Claude Opus, Grok, and ChatGPT to break down complex topics, develop perspectives, and translate raw insights into clean research output, especially when combined with file upload functionality. Crypto-Native Research ToolStack Most general-purpose large models fail in crypto research. The truly important data is scattered across blockchains, niche Twitter posts, live dashboards, Discord groups, and paid reports. If the model cannot understand and reason about this fragmented information, you end up with superficial summaries that sound confident but are actually quite ignorant. This is why I've become picky about AI tools. Truly important tools don't just aggregate. They reason with cryptographic logic. I've spent time testing platforms that prioritize understanding over paraphrasing. SurfAI is an assistant built specifically for crypto research. It's trained on on-chain data, market structure, social sentiment, and a deep, crypto-specific search layer. The result is that it feels more like a smart terminal than a chatbot. A key difference is its distinction between quick queries and genuine research. You can ask simple questions for quick answers or trigger more in-depth reports that combine price movements, fund flows, derivatives, sentiment, and narrative context into truly usable content. I understand why institutions and independent researchers alike are turning to it. This product has a clear understanding of the key elements of the crypto space, and that's a significant advantage. Surf has two distinct operating modes, and I'm impressed by this simplicity. Ask prioritizes speed. It's single-round, lightweight, and direct. I use it for quick queries, such as prices, short summaries, or basic facts, and it responds within a minute. Research is Surf's mode for in-depth analysis. It integrates price movements, on-chain liquidity, derivatives data, and real-time social sentiment into structured reports. This mode is designed for multi-layered reasoning, narrative analysis, strategy development, and full-fledged token economics or macro-level in-depth research. My favorite aspect is that I can control the depth. I can request beginner-level explanations or a concise five-point summary, all through the equally familiar chat interface on the web or mobile. This balance between speed and depth makes Surf impressive.

        Minara

        Minara feels like an early version of a crypto-native virtual CFO. It reads signals that researchers are interested in but translates them into structured analysis and executable workflows.

        The most outstanding feature is automation. Cross-chain transactions can be completed without manual cross-chain operations. Strategies are executed using natural language. There's also an agent system that lets you build monitoring, trading, and yield-related workflows without writing code. When I tested it, Minara didn't just answer questions. It reasoned like someone who truly understands how the crypto market works.

        Minara has three clearly defined operating modes.

        In Chat Mode, I can have natural, high-signal conversations about markets, tokens, protocols, and strategies. It understands the crypto context, not just general financial language.

        Trade Mode (currently on the waiting list) transforms the chat interface into an execution layer. Orders can be placed directly on a perpetual decentralized exchange from the conversation, integrating analysis and action into a single process.

        Minara has three clearly defined operating modes.

        In Chat Mode, I can have natural, high-signal conversations about markets, tokens, protocols, and strategies. It understands the crypto context, not just general financial language.

        Trade Mode (currently on the waiting list) transforms the chat interface into an execution layer. Orders can be placed directly on a perpetual decentralized exchange from the conversation, integrating analysis and action into a single process.

        ... Workflow Mode is what truly impressed me about Minara. It functions like an AI-native financial operating system. I can set price alerts, monitor wallets and tokens, and automate repetitive strategies like dollar-cost averaging. After stress testing with traders, researchers, and miners on token research, fundamental analysis, and trading setups, its key difference became clear: Minara uses cryptographic logic for reasoning, not abstract concepts. Claude Opus, on the other hand, leans more towards language and reasoning than execution. Its strength lies in clearly explaining complex cryptographic systems, making it particularly suitable for newer users. It excels at integration and narrative construction but is weaker in real-time decision support. I see it as a thinking partner rather than a trading or monitoring tool. As a multi-functional AI model, OPUS has achieved excellent performance through deep learning technology and has established a firm foothold in the crypto market. This design, which combines technological innovation with research goals, makes OPUS a representative project driving the integration of artificial intelligence and blockchain technology. ChatGPT also performs excellently as a general-purpose model. Its real advantage is compression. I used it to summarize the white paper, extract core arguments, stress test viewpoints, and reconstruct ideas from different perspectives. With the right cue words and external data, it can be a powerful research co-pilot. While not crypto-native when used alone, it's difficult to replace as part of the broader toolchain. A practical workflow using ChatGPT includes: [Image of ChatGPT tool] I've found that AI tools are far more helpful than harmful to cryptographic research, but only if you use them as amplifiers of judgment, not oracles. When you use them this way, they save time and sharpen thinking. Otherwise, they can mislead you. AI still lacks nuance. It might fabricate numbers. Even when the logic is sound, I've seen models fabricate metrics or misrepresent on-chain data and indicators. If you rely too heavily on opaque outputs, you inherit their blind spots. But the bigger failure pattern is disguising boredom as insight. Some tools produce superficial summaries. They seem useful, but they mislead you with generalities and garbage conclusions. I no longer believe anything that can't be verified. Speed ​​matters. Analytics matters. Source aggregation matters. In general, as long as you do the verification, the benefits outweigh the risks. The best traders and researchers I know still check original sources, pay attention to citations, and combine AI with personal due diligence. That's the advantage. What's really changing is the work itself. Crypto research is shifting from information gathering to signal extraction. Data is scattered across chains, platforms, and social layers. The advantage today comes from systems that can reason in real time through this chaos. More importantly, there's an upper limit that we haven't discussed enough. Recent research by Anthropic highlights the inverse scaling phenomenon of computational load during testing. The paradox is disturbing: giving a model more processing time may actually worsen its inference performance. I've already seen this in real-world applications: overthinking leads to decision degradation. This is a silent warning against assuming "more computing power will always be useful." The lesson I've repeatedly learned is simple: AI is powerful, even intelligent. But it's fragile. Used with discipline and systems thinking, it's a power multiplier. Used carelessly, it's noise. The advantage lies not in automation, but in the ability to quickly validate and apply judgments.

data.title: Buffett's rare stock purchases before retirement have backfired. What signal does this send?
data.description:
For the three years before his retirement, Buffett was a net seller of stocks. The few new positions he finally acquired have all suffered consecutive setbacks since 2026. For example, UnitedHealth Group's stock price plummeted by 20% in a single day.

Today, POOL, which Buffett had been continuously increasing his holdings in, also suffered a setback.

Now, the small opportunities that Buffett sees as potential are not opportunities at all, but rather traps.It's no wonder he's been hoarding so much cash.

Because the entire US stock market is now like AI, built on a foundation of "circular financing"... What happened? Berkshire Hathaway's investment in Pool Corporation began in the third quarter of 2024. In that quarter, Berkshire first disclosed its newly established position in Pool to the SEC, purchasing 404,057 shares. Based on market trading data, the average closing price of Pool stock that quarter was approximately $345.95. Entering the fourth quarter of 2024, Berkshire Hathaway continued its steady investment plan, increasing its holdings by 194,632 shares, a 48.17% increase from the end of the previous quarter, bringing its total holdings to 598,689 shares. In the first quarter of 2025, POOL's stock price declined significantly, and Berkshire Hathaway significantly increased its holdings by 865,311 shares, a substantial increase of 144.53%, rapidly raising its total holdings to 1,464,000 shares. In the second quarter of 2025, POOL's stock price further broke down, falling below the $300 mark. Berkshire Hathaway executed its largest single-quarter purchase since inception: regulatory filings show that Berkshire Hathaway increased its holdings by a staggering 1,994,885 shares in the second quarter of 2025, a surge of 136.26% quarter-over-quarter. This brought Berkshire Hathaway's total holdings in POOL to a record high of 3,458,885 shares, making it one of the company's top 25 holdings and giving Berkshire Hathaway ownership of 8.24% of POOL's total outstanding shares. After four consecutive quarters of buying and maintaining its holdings unchanged in the third quarter of 2025, Berkshire Hathaway's strategy underwent a subtle reversal at the end of 2025. An SEC 13F filing for the fourth quarter of 2025, disclosed in early 2026, revealed that Berkshire reduced its position in POOL for the first time, selling 390,000 shares, representing 11.28% of its total holdings at that time. It is noteworthy that this reduction occurred in an environment of extremely low stock prices. In the fourth quarter of 2025, POOL's average closing price was only $259.34, fluctuating between $228.75 and $312.05. Before reducing its position, Berkshire Hathaway's weighted average cost basis for its Pool Corporation holdings was approximately $320. Having just made a large-scale investment, Berkshire Hathaway drastically reduced its position at a loss, clearly "admitting its mistake." The latest financial report from Pool Corporation seems to confirm this decision: On February 19, 2026, Pool Corporation announced its fourth-quarter and full-year 2025 results, showing revenue, earnings per share (EPS), and forward guidance all falling short of Wall Street expectations, triggering a sharp sell-off. The stock price once fell to a new 52-week low of $216.32, already 30% below Buffett's cost basis. What does this mean? POOL's competitive position remains relatively solid: POOL's gross margin improved by 70 basis points to 30.1% in the fourth quarter, while its full-year underlying gross margin also improved by 20 basis points (a strong gross margin is one of the key indicators of strong competitiveness in the industry), and the company is still expanding: by the end of 2025, POOL's sales center network will expand to 456 directly operated outlets, up from 448 at the end of 2024. However, the company's revenue and profits have not yet stopped declining: net sales for the full year of 2025 were $5.289 billion, continuing to decline from $5.311 billion in 2024; net profit for the full year of 2025 further decreased by 6.4% to $406.4 million, and diluted earnings per share (EPS) continued to fall from $11.30 to $10.85. This is mainly due to continued inflationary pressure: sales and administrative expenses increased by 4% year-on-year to $992.3 million. With declining revenue, this increase in expenses significantly compressed the operating profit margin. Even worse is the deterioration in cash flow quality: operating cash flow plummeted 44% from $659.2 million in 2024 to just $365.9 million in 2025. Free cash flow (excluding capital expenditures) also fell from approximately $600 million to approximately $310 million. POOL's deteriorating performance, balance sheet, and even cash flow and prospects, while possessing characteristics inherent to its industry, also reflect the fragility of the current US economy as a facet of discretionary consumption: POOL's weakness is more easily understood within the context of the increasingly "top-heavy" US consumption structure: According to Moody's Analytics, the top 10% of earners in the US now contribute approximately 49% of all consumer spending—the highest level since this proportion began being tracked in 1989. Meanwhile, the bottom 80%'s share of consumption has fallen to a record low of only 41%. Now, only 10-20% of Americans can be considered truly middle class, enjoying a consumption proportion commensurate with their income level and number. Because the United States is a consumption-driven economy, its economy is now heavily influenced by the spending power of the wealthy. It is estimated that the top 10% of earners alone contribute about one-third of GDP. As for the bottom 80% of earners, their spending growth over the past six years has barely kept pace with inflation—meaning their standard of living has essentially stagnated since the start of the pandemic. Meanwhile, the US Gini coefficient is at a 60-year high, and the confidence gap between high- and low-income groups has widened to its widest level in over a decade. If the spending of the upper-middle class and wealthy, represented by pools, is shrinking, cracks are appearing in other areas as well: A survey in the third quarter of 2025 found that 20% of wealthy and high-net-worth individuals plan to reduce their spending on designer fashion; Bank of America (BofA) credit card data shows that US luxury goods spending has declined for 10 consecutive quarters. Of course, compared to superficial phenomena like pool and luxury goods spending, the real source of influence on the spending of wealthy Americans is the US stock market: the power supporting the consumption of wealthy Americans mainly comes from the "wealth effect" brought about by the stock market—the top 20% of US household wealth holds (directly and indirectly) approximately 87% of corporate stock assets. This has created a disturbing dependency: once the stock market experiences a prolonged correction of more than 10%, the impact will precisely target the group with the highest concentration of consumer spending power. Therefore, the overall US economy is now somewhat like AI's "circular financing": stock market rises – wealth effect – wealthy people consume – boost corporate profits and the economy – stock market continues to rise… However, this prosperity, solely maintained by the wealth effect and fiscal stimulus, will one day collapse, and the performance of discretionary consumer companies has already flashed warning signs.

data.title: The next step in AI: Intelligent Agents
data.description:
Authors: Liu Jin, Yang Yan, Du Runsheng

Human intelligence has three aspects: information collection, information processing to generate cognition, and action based on cognition. Currently, the main application of large language models is in chatbots like ChatGPT, with capabilities concentrated in the first two aspects. However, more useful machine intelligence goes beyond "understanding" and "speaking." If it can "do things" for us like a skilled individual or a group of talented people, it can obviously create greater value. This requires AI agents.

An agent is AI that can do things. LLM is the most important development in the field of AI in recent years. It has already demonstrated extraordinary capabilities in language understanding and generation, dialogue interaction, and knowledge integration, but it is a "brain in a vat"—good at thinking, analyzing, and answering questions, but unable to actually do things.

An agent is AI that can do things.

In the real world, most cognitive activities go beyond simply "providing answers"; they require a complete "cognition-action" loop. We demand that AI autonomously break down complex needs, plan processes, and utilize tools and resources to achieve a complete cycle from perception to decision-making to execution. Furthermore, we hope that AI's actions can extend beyond the realm of computers and the internet, performing tasks for us in the physical world. This requires AI to perceive signals from the physical world, engage in embodied thinking, and translate decisions into execution through devices/robots, directly impacting the real environment. To illustrate, an LLM (Limited Learning Model) is like "Zhuge Liang before he entered the world," skilled in analysis, engaging in strategic discussions with Liu Bei using the "Longzhong Plan," but limited to theoretical discussions. An intelligent agent, on the other hand, is like "Zhuge Liang after he entered the world," possessing comprehensive intelligence, strategizing, organizing resources, deploying troops, and personally leading an expedition to the north. Intelligent agents, using cutting-edge models like LLMs as their brains, are engineered through software to perform complex tasks driven by higher-order goals. It can be said that most complex AI applications in the future will use agents as their carriers. In fact, the AI ​​images we see in science fiction, such as Jarvis in *Iron Man* or HAL 9000 in *2001: A Space Odyssey*, are the creators' intuitive imagination of future AI using agents as carriers. However, AI that interacts with the physical world is extremely important and complex, and this part is now customarily discussed separately in the field of embodied intelligence/robotics. The Composition of Intelligent Agent Capabilities To exert significant value in applications across various industries, an ideal intelligent agent needs to possess several key conditions. First, intelligent agents should possess strong goal understanding and planning capabilities to demonstrate the autonomy of intelligence. Ideally, humans only need to provide an abstract goal, and the intelligent agent can understand the goal, break down the task, plan actions, and complete the execution loop with minimal human intervention. Like the machine TARS in the movie *Interstellar*, in an emergency, it can autonomously assess the situation, formulate and adjust action strategies based on the goal of "saving the astronauts," and even make decisions to sacrifice its own data to complete the mission. This requires machine intelligence to have deep "understanding/thinking" abilities (reasoning, planning, decision-making), the ability to make sharp decisions, and the ability to dynamically adjust task planning based on execution results and environmental feedback, rather than rigidly executing a predetermined path. Second, we hope that intelligent agents can "take action": execution and interaction capabilities. This means that it can use tools, perform operations, and interact directly with the external environment. In the digital world, intelligent agents can simulate human operations, completing tasks through keyboard input and screen clicks; they can also invoke tools through other programs or system interfaces; and they can leverage model strengths to achieve goals by writing and executing code. In the physical world, intelligent agents need to connect to various control systems and devices, manipulating physical objects by issuing commands and translating intelligent decisions into real-world actions. Third, we hope that intelligent agents possess excellent memory and learning capabilities. Memory and state management capabilities are prerequisites for completing long-term, complex tasks. In consumer-facing scenarios, such as personalized schedule management or long-term service support, intelligent agents need to remember user preferences, historical interactions, and long-term states across sessions to reduce repetitive communication and improve service quality. In enterprise-level applications, such as cross-cycle project management and complex business process advancement, intelligent agents need to remember task progress, intermediate results, and key decision-making basis to ensure the continuity of tasks during long-term, multi-stage execution and prevent deviation from the established goals. The significance of learning ability lies in our desire for intelligent agents to continuously improve, evolving from novices to experts through experience and knowledge absorption, much like human employees. Finally, intelligent agents also need strong reliability and controllability to generate value at scale. This is reflected both in the agent's ability to complete tasks stably and robustly, and in whether its behavior consistently aligns with human intentions and values. In *2001: A Space Odyssey*, the AI's choice to sacrifice human crew members to complete the mission is an extreme consequence of the objective function failing to effectively align with human values. As the intelligence and autonomy of intelligent agents increase, the risks of such alignment failures may be further amplified. How to Build Intelligent Agents? The basic logic is simple: using the most "intelligent" and ideal model available as the core (brain), a system is built through software engineering to compensate for the model's shortcomings and approximate the ideal form of an intelligent agent as closely as possible. Large models are currently the optimal choice for the brain of an intelligent agent because their trillions of parameters compress the massive amounts of knowledge accumulated by humans, possess powerful pattern recognition and generation capabilities, serve as a universal interface for processing various unstructured data, including language, and have good generalization capabilities, forming the foundation for handling various tasks. Furthermore, next-generation inference models, represented by OpenAI o1/DeepSeek R1, further propel the development of intelligent agents: enhanced reasoning capabilities lead to stronger task decomposition and planning, better self-checking and error correction, and allow intelligent agents to use tools more accurately. Large models have some structural weaknesses that directly limit the application value of intelligent agents in real-world business scenarios. Therefore, a core task of intelligent agent engineering is to use engineering methods to address these shortcomings, set boundaries, and constrain behavior around the model. First, large models themselves are not very reliable: they suffer from persistent illusions, outdated knowledge, and often irrational task breakdown and planning, lacking systematic verification mechanisms for specific tasks. As a result, the usability of intelligent agents built upon them is significantly reduced: the agent pushes the model from "dialogue" to "action," and errors are no longer just incorrect answers but potential operational risks. Real-world business tasks are often cross-system and long-chain; a small error can amplify at each stage, leading to a persistently high failure rate for long-chain tasks (for example, with a 95% success rate for a single step, the overall success rate for a 20-step chain is only about 36%). To address this, agent engineering typically employs several methods to enhance the reliability of large models by adding an "exoskeleton": introducing retrieval and knowledge bases (RAGs) to mitigate the impact of illusions and outdated knowledge; pre-designing and constraining workflows, rather than allowing completely free "autonomous agents," to limit acceptable execution paths; identifying and filtering high-risk outputs through multiple responses, self-consistency checks, or cross-validation between models; and implementing human approval at critical nodes to ensure that humans have the final say on high-risk actions. Secondly, large models have inherent limitations in their memory capacity: while they "memorize" a vast amount of knowledge during training, they do not continuously learn or "memorize" new knowledge during use after training. During each inference, they can only rely on a finite-length context window to "memorize" information about the current task (different models have different upper limits; content exceeding the window will be forgotten), and cannot naturally maintain stable, long-term individual memory like humans. However, in real-world business scenarios, we need machine intelligence with strong memory capabilities. For example, an AI teacher needs to continuously remember students' learning history, weaknesses, and preferences to truly achieve "personalized instruction" in subsequent explanations and exercises. To address these memory deficiencies, intelligent agent engineering typically employs the following enhancement methods: building an external memory bank to store user preferences, business knowledge, and historical interactions, allowing the intelligent agent to retrieve relevant information on demand through a retrieval mechanism, thus addressing long-term memory deficiencies; summarizing and compressing excessively long contexts, retaining core information, and freeing up token space to address short-term memory forgetting caused by information overload. Besides addressing these shortcomings, because intelligent agents need to act and interact with the environment, they require perception and execution. Firstly, large models themselves cannot actively perceive; they can only passively respond to input. Intelligent agents need to use external perception components to actively acquire environmental information. For tasks in the digital world, agent engineering can be used to create time-based triggers to periodically check logs, emails, stock price fluctuations, etc.; or event-based subscriptions and listening to receive event notifications pushed by APIs, or automatically waking up and recording data when database changes occur. In the physical world, agents can also collect visual, auditory, and tactile signals through devices such as sensors, cameras, and microphones. Secondly, large models lack inherent execution capabilities and require agent engineering to translate intentions into actual actions. Tool invocation is currently the most mainstream approach. Large models generate structured function call instructions based on task requirements, which are then parsed and executed by the intelligent agent framework, such as calling a weather API, querying a database, or sending an email. Another approach is to simulate human operation, using visual recognition and simulated actions like "looking at the screen, clicking buttons, and filling out forms" to complete tasks. The recently popular Doubao phone uses this method to perform intelligent agent operations. For more complex tasks, intelligent agents can also be configured with code interpreters (Code Interpreter/Sandbox), allowing the model to run programmed code, which greatly expands the boundaries of intelligent agent actions. The current capabilities of intelligent agents are largely defined by the year 2025, which has just passed and is often referred to by AI practitioners and tech media as the "Year Zero of Intelligent Agents." This observation is quite accurate. Thanks to the maturity of the following conditions, the development of intelligent agents has entered a fast track in the past year or so: First, the continuous progress of large models, mainly reflected in the emergence of inference models that provide stronger task understanding and planning capabilities, and the development of multimodal models that provide a foundation for intelligent agents to process and generate more complex information. Secondly, the maturity of infrastructure and ecosystem, including open-source frameworks such as LangChain and AutoGPT, has formed a standardized development paradigm after two years of iteration, greatly shortening the development cycle; the popularization of low-code/no-code platforms such as Dify and Coze allows business personnel who do not understand code to quickly generate a dedicated intelligent agent by dragging and dropping; it is worth mentioning that the MCP (Model Context Protocol) and skills (skill system) released by Anthropic in 2025 have provided important standards and inspiration for the intelligent agent ecosystem: MCP, as an open-source protocol standard, makes the interaction between large models and external data sources or tools more unified and convenient, while Skills packages the capabilities/workflows required by humans to complete a certain type of task, allowing agents to work more stably on such tasks. Although the technical content is not high, it has strong practicality at present. Furthermore, both academia and industry have invested significant talent and resources in the field of intelligent agents. For example, at ACL (Association for Computational Linguistics) 2025, over 230 papers were related to intelligent agents, the highest number ever, covering areas such as planning, tool usage, multi-agent collaboration, and evaluation. Driven by improved capabilities, a robust ecosystem, and increased resource investment, various industries are attempting to truly utilize intelligent agents. According to McKinsey's 2025 Global Survey, approximately 62% of surveyed organizations have experimented with agents in some of their business operations (23% have deployed agents at scale in at least one scenario, and 39% are pilot applications). However, looking at specific adoption data across business functions, the industry's application of agents is still in its early stages. The survey indicates that the functions most frequently using agents are, in order, IT, knowledge management, marketing, and service. Taking IT as an example, only 2% and 8% of surveyed companies have fully scaled and scaled their agent deployments, respectively, while 6% and 7% have piloted and experimented with agent deployments. This low level of scaled application is due to two main reasons: first, the capabilities of agents, as discussed earlier, are rapidly improving, but still far from full practicality; second, enterprise users across various industries need certain conditions to effectively utilize agents. Firstly, besides applications in data analysis, generation, and retrieval for short task chains, the large-scale application scenarios of intelligence can be broadly categorized into two types. One is in the programming field, where programming serves as the ideal "training ground" for intelligent agents. It offers environmental isolation, high fault tolerance, clear objectives, compatibility with current planning capabilities, executable programs, and immediate execution feedback. This makes it the first large-scale, commercial breakthrough for intelligent agents. Secondly, specialized intelligent agents in various industries (sales, customer service, human resources, etc.) can be grouped into a large category, sharing a common characteristic: currently, they are primarily workflow automation types. This is actually a stopgap measure to address the insufficient deep understanding (planning, decision-making) capabilities of intelligent agents. By reducing the openness of the agent's tasks, providing reference workflows, and defining a limited set of usable tools, the quality of the agent's work in these tasks can be improved. Further large-scale application of intelligent agents requires their capability evolution to bring tangible value to enterprises. Secondly, for enterprises to effectively utilize intelligent agents, organizational and resource alignment is crucial. According to Anthropic's latest survey in 2026, 46% of respondents indicated that integration with existing systems was the main obstacle to intelligent agent deployment; 43% and 42% cited implementation costs and data accessibility/quality, respectively; and 40% and 39% cited security/compliance and employee learning costs/resistance as significant obstacles (SMEs are particularly concerned about learning costs, with 51% of respondents pointing to this). While cost, security, and technological advancements are largely related to enterprise concerns, data issues, integration problems, and learning/talent issues all require improvement through organizational transformation, further digitalization, and company-wide learning. Summary and Outlook In the future, intelligent agents will be the primary vehicle for applying artificial intelligence across various industries and scenarios. It is foreseeable that with advancements in model capabilities and agent engineering, along with improvements in enterprise data governance and organizational adaptation, intelligent agents will gradually become highly competitive digital employees for every enterprise, competing and collaborating with human employees. From a path perspective, as mentioned earlier, the current large-scale application of intelligent agents focuses on programming and workflow automation. With the improvement of machine intelligence's deep understanding, it is expected that the application of intelligent agents will continuously expand its boundaries, capable of undertaking more abstract and complex tasks, and making more autonomous planning and decisions to transform human intentions into results. Of course, breakthroughs do not mean abandoning workflows. In high-risk enterprise scenarios, workflows/permissions/auditing will become "guardrails" to limit the action space of intelligent agents and ensure application security. For a considerable period, human approval and auditing may remain indispensable in the closed loop of intelligent agent work. Another key direction for the development of intelligent agents is to become human "personal assistants" or "intelligent agents." Compared to other types of intelligent agents, this direction has a higher barrier to entry because it requires stronger personalization capabilities, long-term memory, cross-scenario generalization capabilities, and stricter security boundaries. Personal assistant-type intelligent agents have the potential to reshape the way humans interact with the world—whether it's shopping, socializing, or information acquisition—and may have a disruptive impact on the business logic of many industries. The launch of the "Doubao Phone" in the past year, and the explosive popularity of "OpenClaw" in early 2026, have given us some exciting early explorations of assistant-type intelligent agents. The former represents an attempt at device-level intelligent agents: it deeply embeds intelligent agent capabilities into the operating system, simulating human operations to invoke various apps and complete tasks for users. The latter is an always-on solution that can proactively observe and execute tasks, and acquire rich personalized data and software control through local deployment, initially demonstrating some core characteristics of personal assistant-type agents. According to IDC's forecast, the number of active intelligent agents will climb from approximately 28.6 million in 2025 to 2.216 billion in 2030. This means that in five years, the number of digital workers capable of helping businesses or individuals perform tasks will be nearly 80 times what it is now, with a compound annual growth rate of 139%; the number of tasks performed will surge from 44 billion in 2025 to 415 trillion in 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 524%; and token consumption will skyrocket from 500 billion in 2025 to 1.5 trillion in 2030, with a compound annual growth rate of 34 times. IDC's predictions may not be accurate, but the trend is very clear, and every company should prepare for it.

data.title: Has Trump overplayed his hand? The US Supreme Court rules global tariffs unconstitutional.
data.description:
The shoe has finally dropped! Just now (February 20, 2026), the U.S. Supreme Court issued its final ruling on Trump's most controversial "global tariff" policy since taking office: illegal, and overturned. This legal tug-of-war, which dragged on for months, ended with a temporary setback for the White House. 1. What happened? The core of this ruling is very clear: the president cannot raise taxes at will. The Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that the global tariffs imposed by the Trump administration under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA) constituted an "overreach of authority." The scope of the repeal primarily includes the 10% universal tariffs imposed globally under the guise of "reciprocity" or "anti-drug" measures, as well as some newly imposed emergency tariffs on Canada, Mexico, and China. **Retained Section:** Not all tariffs have disappeared. The tariffs previously imposed on steel, aluminum, and automobiles based on "national security" grounds remain in effect because they are supported by other laws. In short, the Supreme Court held that while the president does have emergency powers, he cannot infinitely extend "adjusting the trade balance" to a "national emergency." 2. Reactions from All Sides: Trump is Furious, Capital Markets Remain Calm The reactions of the White House and the financial markets after the verdict were starkly contrasting: Trump: I Have a "Plan B" Trump immediately took to social media, denouncing the ruling as a "national disgrace." However, he also addressed his supporters: This isn't over yet. In response, Trump announced a 10% tariff increase! His reasoning was that the Supreme Court could not impose tariffs under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act, but under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, tariffs could be imposed on trade deficits, albeit with a 150-day time limit. Section 232, on the other hand, could also impose tariffs based on national security and Section 301 unfair trade practices, but these require prior investigation and determination by the Department of Commerce.
